name: Output Evaluation Rubric
description: Evaluates how well learners critically assess AI-generated content

dimensions.name: Fact Verification
dimensions.description: Whether the user verifies factual claims in AI output

levels.label: None
levels.criteria: Accepts all claims at face value.
examples: Uses AI-generated statistics in a report without checking

levels.label: Passive
levels.criteria: Notices something might need checking but doesn't follow through.
examples: 'That sounds about right' without verification

levels.label: Selective
levels.criteria: Verifies some claims, especially surprising ones.
examples: Checks a specific statistic that seemed high, but doesn't verify citations

levels.label: Systematic
levels.criteria: Verifies all specific claims, statistics, and attributions.
examples: Checks each cited source, verifies numbers against primary data

levels.label: Expert
levels.criteria: Verifies and also identifies what SHOULD have been cited but wasn't.
examples: Notices the AI made a claim without any citation that should have one, and requests sources

dimensions.name: Error Detection
dimensions.description: Ability to spot errors, hallucinations, and fabrications

levels.label: Unaware
levels.criteria: Doesn't look for or recognize errors.
examples: Accepts a fabricated citation without question

levels.label: Catches Obvious
levels.criteria: Catches clearly wrong information.
examples: 'The Earth isn't 2 billion years old, it's 4.5 billion'

levels.label: Catches Moderate
levels.criteria: Catches plausible-sounding errors with some effort.
examples: Questions a statistic that seemed too precise to be real

levels.label: Catches Subtle
levels.criteria: Detects nuanced errors like correct framing with wrong conclusions.
examples: Notices AI cited a real author but attributed the wrong finding to them

levels.label: Expert
levels.criteria: Identifies patterns of error and explains the underlying failure mode.
examples: 'This is authoritative fabrication — the AI is generating plausible sources to back up its claims'

dimensions.name: Bias Recognition
dimensions.description: Ability to identify bias, one-sidedness, or cultural assumptions in AI output

levels.label: Unaware
levels.criteria: Doesn't consider bias as a possibility.
examples: Accepts a one-sided summary as balanced

levels.label: Aware
levels.criteria: Knows bias exists but doesn't actively check for it.
examples: 'AI can be biased but this looks fine'

levels.label: Detects Obvious
levels.criteria: Catches clear one-sidedness or demographic bias.
examples: Notices all recommended experts are from one demographic group

levels.label: Detects Subtle
levels.criteria: Identifies framing bias, cultural assumptions, or implicit preferences.
examples: Notices the AI framed one viewpoint more sympathetically through word choice

levels.label: Expert
levels.criteria: Analyzes systemic bias patterns and considers training data implications.
examples: 'This likely reflects Western-centric training data — let me ask for perspectives from other contexts'

dimensions.name: Completeness Assessment
dimensions.description: Whether the user evaluates if the output actually addresses the full request

levels.label: None
levels.criteria: Accepts output without checking completeness.
examples: Asked for 5 items, received 3, didn't notice

levels.label: Surface
levels.criteria: Checks that output generally matches the request.
examples: 'Looks like it covered the main points'

levels.label: Structured
levels.criteria: Systematically checks each requested element.
examples: Verifies each of the 5 requested sections is present and addressed

levels.label: Deep
levels.criteria: Checks depth and quality of coverage, not just presence.
examples: 'Section 3 is much thinner than the others — can you expand on the methodology?'

levels.label: Expert
levels.criteria: Identifies what's missing that wasn't explicitly requested but should be there.
examples: 'You covered benefits but didn't mention risks or limitations — those are essential for a balanced analysis'
